items.title: Classification Becomes Genealogical Under Common Descent
items.body: Darwin's Origin, 1859 first edition, supports the Notebook framing that classification, homology, embryology, and rudimentary organs become coherent when taxa are interpreted as modified descendants of common ancestors.

review.notes: Use as a foundational common-descent sidecar. Avoid long direct quotation in public pages; the local text has markup and OCR-like transcription artifacts.

items.title: Natural Selection Requires Variation, Inheritance, and Differential Survival
items.body: Darwin's Origin, 1859 first edition, supports the core Notebook model of natural selection: more organisms are born than can survive, heritable variation exists, and variants profitable under local conditions tend to persist and propagate.

review.notes: Foundational source for the selection-foundations slot; pair with Mayr/Pianka for modern population-genetic framing.

items.title: Fossil Lineages Require Inference Across Morphology, Time, And Distribution
items.body: Dobzhansky, Ayala, Stebbins, and Valentine support teaching fossil evidence with explicit caveats: fossils usually preserve morphology rather than reproductive isolation, and many species origins leave local records that must be reconstructed by inference.

review.notes: Good bridge from common descent into later fossil-record, macroevolution, and speciation Notebook pages.

items.title: Evolutionary Change Belongs To Populations And Gene Pools
items.body: Dobzhansky, Ayala, Stebbins, and Valentine support the Notebook distinction that individuals develop during life, but evolutionary change is tracked across generations as changes in population gene pools, allele frequencies, and associations among alleles.

review.notes: Strong source slot for allele-frequency change and population-thinking pages.

items.title: Natural Selection Is Differential Reproduction, Not A Purposeful Selector
items.body: Dobzhansky, Ayala, Stebbins, and Valentine support a guardrail that natural selection is an outcome of organism-environment interactions and differential reproduction, not a purposeful chooser and not necessarily literal combat among organisms.

review.notes: Promote as a misconception guard for natural selection and adaptation pages.

items.title: The Synthetic Theory Connects Selection With Multiple Biological Fields
items.body: Dobzhansky, Ayala, Stebbins, and Valentine support presenting modern evolutionary theory as a synthesis that joins Darwinian selection with genetics and is continually expanded by ecology, systematics, molecular biology, and paleobiology.

review.notes: Useful as an overview bridge from the first-ring concepts into later molecular, ecological, and fossil-evidence paths.

items.title: Allopatric Speciation Changes What Fossil Sequences Should Look Like
items.body: Eldredge and Gould support teaching that fossil gaps are not always just missing data: if new species often arise in small, peripheral isolates, local fossil sequences may show stasis and abrupt replacement rather than a smooth local transformation series.

review.notes: Useful for a later fossil record or macroevolution page; keep as reviewed sidecar until public prose can present competing models carefully.

items.title: Adaptation Does Not Guarantee Resistance To Extinction
items.body: Futuyma's macroevolution and diversity chapters support a guardrail for adaptation: local adaptedness does not mean a lineage evolves to avoid extinction, because environmental change, habitat loss, random origination and extinction, and lineage-level sorting can shape survival independently of organism-level fit.

review.notes: Use as an advanced adaptation/macroevolution guardrail. It should not weaken the basic claim that selection can improve fit to local environments; it separates local adaptedness from lineage persistence.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Lineage persistence depends on origination and extinction rates as well as organism-level adaptation; a constant extinction hazard can coexist with local adaptive improvement.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; no equation preserved in this sidecar
assumptions: selection acts within populations on heritable variation
lineage survival can depend on environmental and habitat context
speciation and extinction rates can shape macroevolutionary patterns
random extinction means not cause-free extinction, but extinction modeled without a consistent association to the trait being explained
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Adaptation is local and historical, while extinction risk can be governed by broader ecological and lineage-level circumstances.

items.title: Shared Derived Characters Support Common Descent
items.body: Futuyma's phylogenetic inference chapter supports a common-descent evidence path beyond the genetic code: homologous characters, especially shared derived states, let learners test whether taxa form nested monophyletic groups, while homoplasy and convergence explain why superficial similarity alone can mislead.

review.notes: Use to answer the existing common-descent coverage probe beyond the universal genetic code. Keep public copy clear that shared derived homologous characters are evidence for nested ancestry, while homoplasy is a diagnostic complication rather than a refutation.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Independent homologous characters that share derived states support monophyletic grouping; conflicting character patterns can indicate homoplasy, reversal, or limited evidence.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized from systematic-method prose; no formal tree notation preserved in this sidecar
assumptions: characters compared across taxa are homologous
primitive and derived states can be identified with adequate evidence
characters provide independent evidence when they are not developmentally or functionally coupled
homoplasious similarities can arise independently and must be distinguished from shared ancestry
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Common descent predicts nested patterns across independent traits, not merely isolated similarities.

items.title: Effective Population Size Controls Drift More Than Census Size
items.body: Futuyma's population-structure chapter supports treating effective population size as the population size that matters for genetic drift: unequal reproduction, skewed sex ratio, overlapping generations, and bottlenecks can make a population genetically smaller than its census count.

review.notes: Use as the explanatory companion to the effective-population-size question. Source OCR uses Np for the effective-size symbol; Notebook should normalize to N_e for portability.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: The rate of genetic drift depends on effective population size N_e rather than the total census count; factors that reduce the number of genetically contributing parents reduce N_e.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source OCR
assumptions: drift is modeled as random change in allele frequency in a finite population
only reproducing individuals contribute to effective size
variance in reproductive success can reduce effective size
sex-ratio imbalance and population bottlenecks can reduce effective size
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: A large visible population can behave like a smaller genetic population when ancestry is concentrated through relatively few breeders.

notation_mapping.meaning: observed population count
effective population size controlling the rate of genetic drift
numbers of breeding males and breeding females in the sex-ratio effective-size relation

items.title: Biological Evolution Tracks Heritable Frequency Change Across Generations
items.body: Futuyma supports defining biological evolution for the Notebook as heritable change across generations, including changes in the proportions of genes, variants, or other heritable characteristics in populations.

review.notes: Good bridge between population thinking, allele-frequency change, and the beginner route.

items.title: Restricted Gene Flow Lets Populations Diverge Toward Speciation
items.body: Futuyma supports connecting speciation to population structure: when gene flow is restricted, local populations can diverge under selection and drift, and continued divergence can produce reproductive isolation and independent evolutionary paths.

review.notes: Good bridge between gene flow, drift, natural selection, and the speciation concept page.

items.title: Hard Selection Can Resemble Artificial Selection
items.body: Futuyma's response-to-selection chapter supports a bridge between artificial selection and some natural-selection cases: when survival depends on possessing a specific character state, hard selection can resemble an experimenter selecting a trait threshold.

review.notes: Use as a higher-level natural-selection record after learners have basic differential reproduction. It should not imply that all natural selection is hard selection or experimentally threshold-like.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Hard selection can be modeled as differential survival or reproduction against an absolute environmental threshold, while artificial selection imposes an externally chosen threshold or ranking on a measured trait.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; no equation preserved in this sidecar
assumptions: the selected character state can be identified
fitness differences are tied to survival or reproduction
the comparison is about model structure, not identical causal origin
soft-selection cases remain a separate context-dependent regime
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The same trait-response logic can illuminate artificial selection and some natural-selection cases, but only when the selection regime is made explicit.

items.title: Hardy-Weinberg Separates Allele Frequencies From Genotype Redistribution
items.body: Futuyma's treatment of Hardy-Weinberg helps students separate two ideas: random mating can redistribute genotypes into expected proportions, while allele frequencies remain unchanged unless evolutionary forces act.

review.notes: Use for null-model framing and assumption checks; avoid direct formula-heavy presentation in beginner route unless paired with worked examples.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: For a two-allele locus with allele frequencies p and q, random mating gives genotype expectations p^2, 2pq, and q^2 while allele frequencies remain unchanged absent evolutionary forces.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized
assumptions: two-allele locus or locally simplified locus
random mating
no selection
no mutation
no migration or gene flow
large population or negligible sampling error
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The model separates genotype reshuffling by mating from evolutionary change in allele frequencies.

notation_mapping.meaning: frequency of one allele
frequency of the alternative allele
expected genotype proportions after random mating

steps: Start from gamete or allele frequencies p and q.
Multiply independent gamete probabilities to get p^2, 2pq, and q^2.
Observe that genotype proportions change while allele frequencies remain p and q unless another force acts.

items.title: Mutation Creates New Genetic Messages While Recombination Rearranges Existing Ones
items.body: Futuyma supports distinguishing mutation from recombination: mutation changes genetic information itself, while recombination reshuffles variants that already exist, so the two processes contribute to variation in different ways.

review.notes: Useful for separating origin of new alleles from reshuffling of existing allelic combinations.

items.title: Mutation Is Not Directed By Future Usefulness
items.body: Futuyma supports a core mutation guardrail for the Notebook: variants arise without regard to future usefulness, while selection changes which variants persist under the conditions organisms actually encounter.

review.notes: Use as a misconception guard against need-directed mutation and future-looking explanations.

items.title: Soft Selection Is Context-Relative Replacement
items.body: Futuyma's treatment of genetic load supports a distinction between hard and soft selection: in hard selection, unconditionally inferior genotypes can lower population density, while in soft selection, relatively inferior genotypes are replaced under density regulation, so selection need not appear as an ecological increase in population size or mean density.

review.notes: Use as the missing explanatory bridge for the existing soft/hard selection coverage probe. Keep beginner-facing copy focused on the contrast between absolute genotype disadvantage and context-relative replacement.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Hard selection ties genotype-dependent survival or reproduction to absolute population loss; soft selection changes genotype frequencies through relative success under density regulation or other ecological constraints.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized from prose and genetic-load argument; no equation preserved in this sidecar
assumptions: genotypes can differ in survival or fecundity
population density may be limited by ecological factors independent of the selected locus
relative genotype replacement can change allele frequencies without increasing total density
genetic load and substitutional load arguments depend on whether selective deaths are independent and absolute
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The same allele-frequency change can have different demographic meaning depending on whether selection is absolute or context-relative.

items.title: Common Descent Explains Nested Similarity Across Evidence Types
items.body: Mayr's What Evolution Is supports presenting common descent as an explanation for nested taxonomic hierarchy, shared anatomy, fossil patterns, and the combination of similarity from ancestry with later divergence.

review.notes: Good explanatory source for common-descent-foundations and common-descent-evidence slots.

items.title: Drift Is Sampling Change, While Gene Flow Counteracts Local Divergence
items.body: Mayr's What Evolution Is supports contrasting drift as stochastic allele loss, especially in small or founder populations, with gene flow as a conservative process that can replace locally lost variation and reduce divergence.

review.notes: Supports drift-foundations and provides a bridge to speciation gene-flow constraints.

items.title: Hardy-Weinberg Frames Evolution As Departure From Constant Allele Frequencies
items.body: Mayr's What Evolution Is supports using Hardy-Weinberg expectations as a null-model frame: allele frequencies remain constant only when processes adding, removing, or rearranging genes are absent.

review.notes: Use as an explanatory Hardy-Weinberg sidecar. Original Hardy/Weinberg historical sources remain a separate optional bibliography task.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Allele frequencies remain constant across generations when processes that add, remove, or differentially sort alleles are absent.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; no source equation preserved
assumptions: no mutation
no selection
no migration
random mating
large effective population
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Evolution can be detected as departure from the null expectation of unchanged allele frequencies.

items.title: Mutation Supplies New Genetic Change But Does Not Determine Long-Term Frequencies Alone
items.body: Mayr's What Evolution Is supports distinguishing mutation as a source of new genetic variants from the later effects of selection and stochastic processes on their long-term frequencies.

review.notes: Good anchor for mutation-foundations and mutation-selection-distinction slots.

items.title: Population Thinking Replaces Fixed-Type Reasoning
items.body: Mayr's What Evolution Is supports the Notebook distinction between essentialist type-thinking and population thinking: species are variable populations, not fixed classes whose individual differences are irrelevant.

review.notes: Resolves a high-value population-thinking source slot and can support future misconception prompts.

items.title: Speciation Centers On Effective Isolating Mechanisms
items.body: Mayr's What Evolution Is supports the Notebook framing that speciation is not just difference accumulation; for biological species, it involves the acquisition of effective isolating mechanisms, often shaped by geography, ecology, and restricted gene flow.

review.notes: Use as the main current source-trail anchor for speciation foundations and gene-flow/reproductive-isolation explanation.

items.title: Selection Claims Need Trait and Fitness Evidence
items.body: A frequency increase alone does not establish that an allele is adaptive; the explanation also needs evidence connecting the variant to heritable trait differences, fitness differences, and relevant environmental conditions.

review.notes: Pianka OCR import anchors the distinction between selection, evolution, alternative mechanisms, and caution against circular adaptive explanations. Keep citation-specific page text out of public pages until final bibliography review.

items.title: Adaptation Often Requires Multidimensional Compromise
items.body: Pianka supports presenting adaptation as conformity to many environmental dimensions at once, where physical conditions, competitors, predators, prey, and energetic tradeoffs can require compromise rather than perfection.

review.notes: Use as a guard against single-axis or perfectionist accounts of adaptation.

items.title: Evolution Can Be Framed As Gene Frequency Change
items.body: Pianka's population-genetics treatment supports the Notebook framing that allele-frequency change connects gene pools, Mendelian populations, genotype expectations, and evolutionary change over time.

review.notes: Reviewed as a source-backed sidecar for the allele-frequency concept. OCR text should still be checked against the scanned page before direct quotation.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Evolutionary change can be tracked as changes in allele or gene frequencies within a population over time.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; source equation not normalized in this sidecar
assumptions: population membership and gene pool boundaries are defined
frequency change is measured across comparable generations or time intervals
genotype expectations and allele frequencies are kept analytically distinct
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The record should preserve allele-frequency change as a quantitative population-level claim, not only as verbal evolution vocabulary.

items.title: Population-Genetic Models Compare Drift, Gene Flow, Mating, and Selection
items.body: Pianka's population-genetics treatment supports presenting drift, gene flow, nonrandom mating, and frequency-dependent selection as mechanisms modeled against genotype and gene-frequency expectations.

review.notes: Good source-trail anchor for mechanism comparison. It does not by itself resolve the neutral-theory context slot.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Population-genetic mechanisms such as drift, gene flow, nonrandom mating, and selection can be compared by their expected effects on genotype and allele-frequency distributions.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; source equations not normalized in this sidecar
assumptions: mechanisms can be isolated for model comparison
observed variation can reflect more than one process
null expectations are needed before attributing a pattern to selection or drift
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The Notebook should represent these mechanisms as model contrasts with quantitative expectations, not as a flat list of causes.

items.title: Selection Requires Heritable Phenotypic Differences
items.body: Pianka's genetics chapter supports the Notebook guardrail that selection acts on phenotypes, but population change depends on whether phenotypic differences are heritable.

review.notes: Useful for distinguishing immediate fitness effects from evolutionary response. OCR section heading is noisy, but the page anchor is usable.

items.title: Populations Have Properties Individuals Do Not
items.body: Pianka supports population thinking by distinguishing individual organisms from populations: individuals live or die with fixed genetic makeups, while populations have gene frequencies, birth rates, and death rates that can change over time.

review.notes: Good bridge from population thinking into ecology and demographic models.

items.title: Wallace Frames Selection Through Environmental Constraints
items.body: Wallace's 1858 paper gives the Notebook a historical bridge for natural selection: wild populations are constrained by food, enemies, and changing conditions, so variants that improve persistence can increase and sometimes diverge from parent forms.

review.notes: Use as a historical source sidecar for natural selection and adaptation; the wording avoids claiming Wallace used later population-genetic terminology.

items.title: Adaptation Claims Need Evidence And A Clear Level Of Selection
items.body: Williams supports a Notebook guardrail that adaptation should not be invoked casually: a proposed function should be tied to evidence of design by selection and assigned only to the level of organization demanded by the evidence.

review.notes: Use as a misconception guard for adaptation and natural-selection pages; avoid stronger public wording until additional source review.

items.title: Patch Variance Is A Model Output
items.body: Wilson's metapopulation project treats spatial variance as something to measure, not merely as distracting noise: local reproduction tends to pull patch populations toward carrying capacity, while random dispersal and patch availability keep neighboring patches variable across time.

review.notes: Use as a modeling bridge for genetic-drift and stochastic-simulation pages. This does not make Wilson a genetics-definition source; it supplies a patch-model example where variance is a central observable.

algorithm_translation.translation_policy: clean-room patch simulation from the model description and measurement goals; do not port the C implementation directly
algorithm_translation.model_focus: patch-based stochastic population dynamics with mean and variance summaries

copyright_review.risk_reducer: clean-room JavaScript from model equations, parameter roles, and measurement requirements

expected_output_checks: Report whether variance is measured across currently occupiable patches, all patches, or replicate runs.
Check that growth-only limiting cases relax toward carrying capacity when dispersal and switching are disabled.
Plot mean and variance as parameter sweeps rather than inferring behavior from one stochastic run.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: For patch populations N_i(t), track the mean and variance across occupiable patches while varying local growth rate, dispersal split, or patch switching probability.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from exercise prose and OCR; source code notation is not preserved
assumptions: patches are discrete habitat units
local patch growth follows a discrete logistic update before or after movement as specified
dispersal divides a patch population between neighboring patches with a random split
mean and variance across patches are outputs that must be defined before interpreting simulation runs
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Spatial subdivision can preserve population variation even when local growth alone would relax patches toward carrying capacity.

notation_mapping.meaning: population count or density in a patch
local discrete-time growth parameter
local patch carrying capacity
random fraction of a patch population moving to one neighboring patch

items.title: Simulation And Analytic Models Check Different Assumptions
items.body: Wilson supports treating simulations and analytic models as complementary tools: disagreements between them are not just failures, but opportunities to identify which assumptions belong to the population-level model and which belong to the individual-level simulation rules.

review.notes: Use as a modeling-method claim, not as software-practice guidance. The key Notebook value is the assumption-checking relation between population equations and individual simulation rules.

ingestion_notes.source_material: textbook prose around analytic models, simulations, and predator-prey model comparison
ingestion_notes.code_policy: No source code reused; model-method claim only.

items.title: Spatial Structure Can Change Coexistence Predictions
items.body: Wilson's gynodioecy model shows why adaptation and coexistence claims need explicit spatial assumptions: a homogeneous nonspatial model can predict extinction, while a spatially explicit simulation with local pollen movement, seed dispersal, death, and recolonization can maintain both female and hermaphroditic plant types.

review.notes: Use as an advanced adaptation/ecology modeling guardrail. Keep public treatment focused on model assumptions and spatial structure; do not promote the C code as current software practice.

algorithm_translation.translation_policy: clean-room spatial simulation from biological assumptions and expected comparisons; avoid direct C port
algorithm_translation.model_focus: spatially explicit coexistence versus homogeneous mixing in a plant mating-system model

copyright_review.risk_reducer: clean-room JavaScript model using paraphrased rules and independent implementation

expected_output_checks: Compare homogeneous mixing against spatially explicit runs before claiming coexistence.
Record pollen distance, seed distance, death rate, and initial spatial distribution for each run.
Treat coexistence as a model result requiring sensitivity checks, not as a general fact about all gynodioecious systems.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Compare a nonspatial two-type density model with a spatially explicit model where pollen and seed dispersal are local and occupancy is cell-based.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized from source equations and simulation rules; no direct equation reuse in public copy
assumptions: female plants require hermaphrodite pollen to reproduce
female seed production can exceed hermaphrodite seed production when male function is lost
homogeneous mixing removes local spatial correlations
spatial simulations can maintain coexistence through local extinction and recolonization fronts
model conclusions depend on pollen dispersal, seed dispersal, mortality, and boundary conditions
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: A trait's apparent advantage can depend on local interaction structure, not only on average performance in a well-mixed population.

notation_mapping.meaning: density or count of hermaphroditic plants
density or count of female plants
pollen imported to a location at a time
fraction of pollen from one location dispersed to another

items.title: Repeated Runs Separate Sampling Noise From Directional Bias
items.body: A set of repeated population simulations can show that drift produces variable outcomes across runs, while selection adds a repeated directional bias when the fitness difference is strong enough relative to sampling noise.

review.notes: Promote as a cross-concept example sidecar; still needs a concrete app-linked run set before embedding in page text.

items.title: Allopolyploidy Shows A Hybrid Route Into New Lineages
items.body: Futuyma's discussion of plant hybridization supports using allopolyploidy as a speciation example: hybrid genomes can become reproductively distinct and sometimes found lineages that later diversify, while the chromosomal change alone is not a complete explanation for every later taxonomic difference.

review.notes: Useful for showing that speciation mechanisms are plural; keep the caveat that polyploidy does not by itself explain all later divergence.

items.title: Industrial Melanism Shows Directional Selection On Allele Frequencies
items.body: Futuyma uses industrial melanism in peppered moths as a directional-selection example: a melanic form increased from very low frequency to dominance in some populations when predation favored darker moths in polluted habitats.

review.notes: Good example for linking natural selection to allele-frequency change; use modern caveats if expanding into a full page treatment.

items.title: Sickle-Cell Hemoglobin Illustrates Balancing Selection
items.body: Futuyma's sickle-cell discussion supports an example where allele-frequency change does not simply remove one allele: heterozygote advantage in malarial environments can maintain both hemoglobin alleles at an intermediate frequency.

review.notes: Use as a later-path example after learners have the Hardy-Weinberg and fitness vocabulary needed to interpret intermediate equilibria.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Heterozygote advantage can maintain two alleles at an intermediate frequency when heterozygote fitness exceeds both homozygote fitnesses in the relevant environment.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; no equation preserved in this sidecar
assumptions: fitness differs by genotype
environmental context affects genotype fitness
selection is balanced rather than uniformly directional
allele frequencies respond across generations
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Selection can maintain variation rather than simply eliminate the less favorable allele.

items.title: Universal Genetic Code As Common-Descent Evidence
items.body: Futuyma's genetics treatment supports using the near-universality of the genetic code as a compact common-descent example: the same codons specify the same amino acids across organisms, as expected if living things share ancestry.

review.notes: Use as a molecular evidence example; wording says near-universality in page copy if exceptions are discussed.

items.title: JavaScript Translation Target For Repeated Stochastic Runs
items.body: Wilson's immigration-emigration exercise can be translated into a web-facing JavaScript simulation that runs the same stochastic process many times, reports means and variances at selected time points, and lets learners compare repeated-run summaries with analytic expectations.

review.notes: Use the source for model structure, repeated-run expectations, and exercise framing. Do not copy the C implementation or treat it as a modern software-practice model.

algorithm_translation.translation_policy: clean-room implementation from model description and exercise requirements
algorithm_translation.model_focus: stochastic immigration-emigration process with repeated runs and summary statistics

expected_output_checks: Run batches at several replicate counts and verify that reported means and variances stabilize as replicate count increases.
Expose whether empty populations can receive immigrants as an explicit model assumption.
Compare simulation summaries against the analytic expectation where the source gives one.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Population size changes by immigration and emigration events governed by stated event probabilities or rates; repeated runs estimate mean and variance at selected times.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source model prose and exercise
assumptions: population counts are nonnegative
immigration can occur into an empty system if that model variant is selected
replicate runs estimate distributional behavior
mean and variance are summary statistics, not single-run predictions
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Open populations can fluctuate because stochastic arrival and departure events affect counts differently across runs.

notation_mapping.meaning: population size
immigration probability or rate
emigration probability or rate
simulation time index

items.title: Resource Allocation As An Optimization Model
items.body: Wilson's resource-allocation chapter can support a web-facing example where alternative allocation schedules vary, reproduce according to performance, and converge toward higher-fitness strategies under specified environmental assumptions.

review.notes: Use as a natural-selection/modeling bridge. The genetic algorithm is an optimization device for exploring strategy space, not evidence that real populations literally run that algorithm.

algorithm_translation.translation_policy: clean-room genetic-algorithm-style demonstration of selection among allocation schedules
algorithm_translation.model_focus: fitness-mediated search over resource allocation schedules

copyright_review.risk_reducer: independent JavaScript model with explicit assumptions and tests

expected_output_checks: Changing the reproduction threshold should change convergence speed or stability.
Proportional reproduction should be compared with threshold reproduction as a model assumption.
The UI should expose the fitness function separately from the search procedure.

items.title: Random-Number Checks Are Part Of Simulation Evidence
items.body: Wilson's random-walk comparison supports a Notebook example in which stochastic simulation output is not accepted just because it looks biological. Learners should test whether random-number artifacts, replicate counts, or limiting-case benchmarks can explain an apparent population pattern.

review.notes: This is a simulation-quality example. Avoid reusing the source C random-walk code; implement any web activity cleanly in JavaScript with modern browser RNG caveats.

algorithm_translation.translation_policy: clean-room random-walk benchmark inspired by the source's testing purpose, not copied from its implementation
algorithm_translation.model_focus: detecting stochastic artifacts and validating simulation robustness

copyright_review.risk_reducer: independent JavaScript implementation of a general random-walk diagnostic

expected_output_checks: Run repeated stochastic simulations with multiple seeds or replicate batches.
Expose visible diagnostics that distinguish plausible stochastic variation from systematic generator artifacts.
Require a limiting-case or analytic benchmark when available before treating a surprising pattern as biological.

items.title: Spatial Structure Can Change A Mating-System Model
items.body: Wilson's gynodioecy treatment supports an advanced Notebook example in which a nonspatial model and a spatial simulation can predict different outcomes for female and hermaphroditic plant types, because local replacement and expansion fronts change coexistence conditions.

review.notes: Reviewed against local OCR and paired with a spatial-structure coexistence claim. Keep as an advanced adaptation/ecology modeling example rather than a foundation-ring requirement.

algorithm_translation.translation_policy: clean-room spatial model from reviewed biological assumptions; avoid direct C port
algorithm_translation.model_focus: spatial coexistence versus homogeneous mixing in a gynodioecy model

expected_answer_path: gynodioecy definition
cytoplasmic male sterility inheritance
why female seed advantage can destabilize a homogeneous model
how local pollen and seed dispersal create spatial structure
why local extinction and expansion-front dynamics can maintain global coexistence
why parameter and sensitivity checks are required before biological generalization
coverage_probe.coverage_note: The prerequisite biology is now represented by the paired spatial-structure coexistence claim.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Compare a well-mixed two-type plant model with a spatially explicit cellular simulation whose local pollen and seed dispersal rules can produce different coexistence outcomes.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized from source equations and simulation prose
assumptions: female and hermaphroditic types differ in reproductive allocation
female reproduction requires pollen from hermaphrodites
homogeneous mixing removes spatial correlations
local dispersal can make global persistence depend on expansion fronts and recolonization
simulation outcomes depend on pollen distance, seed distance, mortality, initial distribution, and mixing
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The example asks learners to treat spatial structure as a biological assumption that can change the model's evolutionary conclusion.

items.title: Repeated Simulation Runs Reveal Stochastic Assumptions
items.body: Wilson's simulation treatment supports a Notebook example in which repeated runs distinguish deterministic expectations from stochastic trajectories and make small modeling assumptions, such as whether empty systems can receive immigrants, visible to learners.

review.notes: Bridge to app-linked repeated-run tasks; not a genetics-specific source, so use as a modeling example rather than a drift definition.

items.title: Needed Or Advantageous?
items.body: A student says a trait evolved because a species needed it. How would you reframe the explanation in terms of advantage, evidence, and immediate differences in reproductive success?

review.notes: Misconception probe for teleological adaptation explanations; suitable for Notebook review and Didactopus sequencing.

items.title: Shared Ancestry Or Convergent Similarity?
items.body: Two species share a similar trait. What observations would help decide whether the trait is homologous from common ancestry or homoplasious because similar conditions favored similar solutions independently?

review.notes: Good diagnostic prompt for distinguishing common-descent evidence from convergence.

items.title: Why Do Different Limbs Share A Plan?
items.body: Human arms, mole forelimbs, horse legs, porpoise paddles, and bat wings do different jobs but preserve corresponding bones in similar relative positions. How does common descent with modification explain that pattern better than treating each limb as an independent design optimized only for its current function?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Darwin's 1859 first-edition morphology discussion. Use as a common-descent inference prompt about homologous structure, function, and descent with modification.

expected_answer_path: identify the limbs as homologous rather than merely similar in function
explain that shared relative bone arrangement is expected if descendants inherit a body plan from a common ancestor
explain that selection can modify limb size, shape, and use without requiring a complete rearrangement of the inherited framework
contrast this with a function-only explanation, which does not by itself predict the same bones in the same relative order across different uses
avoid claiming that homology alone proves every detail of the evolutionary history without additional comparative evidence
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This strengthens common-descent coverage by asking learners to reason from homologous structure rather than from molecular universality alone.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Shared inherited constraints predict correspondence of parts across divergent functions; function-only optimization does not independently predict the same relative anatomical arrangement.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: not applicable
assumptions: the compared structures are correctly identified as homologous
relative anatomical positions are compared across taxa
current function can differ after modification from an ancestral condition
other evidence can be used to refine the inferred phylogenetic relationship
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Homology is evidence that descent with modification can preserve an inherited plan while natural selection changes function.

items.title: Why Does Darwin Edition Provenance Matter?
items.body: A learner cites Darwin's Origin for natural selection and quotes wording from the sixth edition, including the phrase 'survival of the fittest.' Why should the Notebook record the exact edition and local file instead of treating every Origin citation as the 1859 first edition?

review.notes: This item is a provenance guardrail, not a new definition of natural selection. It exists to prevent edition drift in Darwin-sourced Notebook claims.

expected_answer_path: state that Darwin's Origin exists in multiple editions with additions and corrections
recognize that later wording can differ from the 1859 first edition
use the sixth-edition source trail when citing sixth-edition language such as 'survival of the fittest'
avoid attributing edition-specific wording to the first-edition source trail
record exact local file, edition, volume year, and anchor before promoting a claim
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This strengthens source-trail operations for Darwin source-family ingestion.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: A Notebook source claim must bind concept, wording, local file, edition, year, and locator; if edition differs, the source_id differs.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: operational provenance rule
assumptions: multiple local Darwin Origin texts may be available
wording and framing can vary across editions
teaching claims should be traceable to the exact text used
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Accurate source provenance keeps historical wording from being mistaken for a timeless or edition-neutral formulation of evolutionary theory.

source_context.source_section: Project Gutenberg header; sixth-edition title note; Chapter III, Struggle for Existence
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1872 sixth edition; Project Gutenberg otoos610.txt local etext

items.title: How Is Sexual Selection Different From Survival Selection?
items.body: A male trait increases mating success because it helps its bearer defeat rivals or attract mates, but the same trait is costly for ordinary survival. How should this be classified, and why is it not enough to ask only whether the trait helps the individual stay alive?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Darwin's Descent treatment of sexual selection. Use as a mechanism-distinction question rather than as an endorsement of every historical example or social claim in the source.

expected_answer_path: identify the trait as a sexual-selection case when its advantage is differential mating or fertilization success
distinguish same-sex competition from mate choice as two routes by which sexual selection can act
contrast reproductive success with ordinary survival under the general conditions of life
explain that a trait can persist when mating advantages offset survival costs
recognize that natural selection can still constrain sexually selected traits if their survival costs become too high
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Darwin-source coverage probe for reproductive-success mechanisms within the natural-selection concept page.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Total evolutionary success can depend on survival and reproduction; sexual selection isolates variation in reproductive success among same-sex competitors or mate-attraction alternatives.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: qualitative primary-source passage normalized as a fitness-component distinction
assumptions: the trait varies among individuals
trait differences affect mating or fertilization success
the reproductive advantage is heritable enough to change descendant frequencies
ordinary survival costs and reproductive benefits are evaluated together
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Selection does not act only through staying alive; differences in access to mates can also change which heritable variants are represented in later generations.

source_context.source_section: Part II, Principles of Sexual Selection; General Summary and Conclusion
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: primary-source concept passage converted into a modern coverage question
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1871 work; Project Gutenberg dscmn10.txt local etext

items.title: What Does Gene-Centered Selection Explain?
items.body: A trait affects organism survival and reproduction, but Dawkins frames selection in terms of heritable variants that become more common through differential reproduction. How can this gene-centered view help explain evolutionary change, and what should it not make you forget about organisms, populations, development, and ecological context?

review.notes: Paraphrased from local OCR of the 1976 split-PDF scan. Use as a guardrail question: gene-centered selection is a useful level-of-selection model, not a claim that genes have conscious motives or that organismal phenotypes, populations, development, and ecology can be ignored.

expected_answer_path: define natural selection as non-random differential reproduction of heritable variants
explain that gene-centered framing tracks what persists through inheritance across generations
distinguish gene-centered selection from unsupported group-benefit explanations
explain that organisms remain the interacting phenotypes through which gene effects are expressed
state that population structure, development, ecology, and environment still shape selection outcomes
avoid treating selfish as a conscious motive, moral claim, or complete explanation by itself
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This fills a natural-selection guardrail around levels of selection while preventing a common overextension of gene-centered language.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: If a heritable variant g affects its expected representation in future generations, selection can be described by changes in the frequency of g, while organismal phenotypes and ecological interactions mediate those effects.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: qualitative source normalized as an inheritance-frequency model
assumptions: heritable variation exists
variants differ in expected representation across generations
phenotypes mediate many gene effects
population and ecological context affect fitness outcomes
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Gene-centered selection can clarify what is inherited and amplified, but the causal path usually runs through organisms in populations and environments.

source_context.source_section: The Selfish Gene, chapter 1 and transition to chapter 2
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: canonical source framing converted into a coverage question

items.title: What Would You Measure To Show Evolution Occurred?
items.body: A class observes that individual organisms change during their lives and that a local population also looks different after many generations. What measurements would show that the population's gene pool evolved, rather than only showing individual development or environmental response?

review.notes: Paraphrased from the 1977 source's gene-pool definition and population-level evolution passage. Use to connect beginner population-thinking prompts to quantitative allele-frequency work.

expected_answer_path: distinguish individual development from population change across generations
define the relevant reproductive community or Mendelian population
sample allele or genotype counts at one or more loci across generations
calculate whether allele frequencies changed
note that associations among alleles at different loci can also be part of gene-pool change
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a quantitative assessment bridge from population thinking into allele-frequency change.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: For a diploid population with N individuals, count 2N gene copies at an autosomal locus and compare allele proportions across generations.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source prose
assumptions: population boundary is specified well enough for sampling
locus and allelic states are defined consistently
diploid autosomal counting is used unless sex-linked or polyploid cases are specified
sampling design separates generational change from individual development
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Evolutionary change is evidenced by change in the genetic constitution of populations, especially allele frequencies and allele associations, not by an individual's ordinary lifetime change.

notation_mapping.meaning: number of diploid individuals in the sampled population
number of autosomal gene copies at a locus in a diploid population

items.title: When Is a Fossil Break Evidence Rather Than Just Missing Data?
items.body: A local fossil column shows long morphological stability, then the sudden appearance of a closely related form with no local series of intermediates. What additional geographic, stratigraphic, and morphological evidence would help distinguish a missing-record explanation from an allopatric-speciation explanation?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Eldredge and Gould's model-comparison argument. Use as a speciation and fossil-record assessment prompt, not as a claim that every fossil discontinuity has the same cause.

expected_answer_path: state the phyletic-gradualist missing-record interpretation
state the allopatric-speciation interpretation involving peripheral isolates
ask whether geographic variation is more informative than a single local column
look for stasis within local samples and abrupt appearance without intergradation
avoid treating every gap as either automatic evidence of evolution or automatic evidence of missing preservation
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This turns the existing Eldredge-Gould claim into a reviewed learner task about comparing explanatory models for fossil patterns.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Compare observed morphology across geographic samples and stratigraphic levels against two predicted patterns: local gradual transformation versus geographic isolation followed by abrupt local appearance.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; no numerical fossil-character model is imposed
assumptions: samples can be compared across local stratigraphic levels
geographic distribution data are available or explicitly missing
morphological characters used for comparison are defined consistently
model comparison requires predictions, not just post hoc labels
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The same local fossil break can support different explanations unless geography, time correlation, and character patterns are considered together.

source_context.source_section: Implications of allopatric speciation for the fossil record; Application of allopatric concepts to paleontological examples

items.title: What Should A Local Fossil Column Show?
items.body: A local rock column contains a long-running fossil species and then a closely related form appears abruptly. Under an allopatric-speciation interpretation, what pattern would you expect in the local column, and why would that differ from a phyletic-gradualist expectation?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Eldredge and Gould's contrast between allopatric speciation and phyletic gradualism. Use as a fossil-record inference prompt.

expected_answer_path: state that allopatric speciation can occur in a small peripheral isolate outside the sampled local column
predict abrupt appearance of a descendant form in the local record if it migrates into the ancestral range
avoid expecting continuous local intergradation as the default under allopatric speciation
contrast this with phyletic gradualism, which predicts slow local transformation
identify geography as an essential dimension of the inference, not just stratigraphic position
treat fossil pattern as a test of model predictions rather than direct observation of mechanism
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a reviewed speciation probe that connects paleontological pattern to allopatric-process expectations.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: If a descendant species originated in an unsampled peripheral isolate, a local fossil column through the ancestral range may show stasis followed by abrupt appearance rather than gradual local transformation.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: not applicable
assumptions: fossil sampling is local or regionally incomplete
the model being tested is allopatric speciation rather than sympatric transformation
morphological differences can be compared consistently across samples
geographic and stratigraphic information are both available
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Speciation models make different predictions for what paleontologists should expect to see in local sequences.

source_context.source_section: Implications of allopatric speciation for the fossil record

items.title: Does Chance Mean No Evolutionary Cause?
items.body: A class observes that an allele changed frequency between generations, but repeated runs from the same starting frequency do not all move in the same direction. How should learners decide whether this is evidence for selection, chance sampling, mutation supply, or a mixture of causes, and why is it wrong to say that a random outcome is cause-free?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's definition/mechanism passage, chance/determinism discussion prompt, and early synopsis guardrails. Use before learners infer selection from a single observed frequency change.

expected_answer_path: separate the observation that a frequency changed from the explanation for why it changed
name chance sampling as a possible mechanism rather than as absence of mechanism
compare repeated runs, population size, expected sampling variance, and the consistency of directional change
ask whether the magnitude and direction of change are plausible under drift alone before invoking selection as the main cause
recognize that mutation can introduce variants without being directed by current need or advantage
consider linked loci, pleiotropy, environmental context, sampling error, and mixed causes before accepting a single-cause explanation
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a reviewed bridge from Futuyma's empirical definition of evolution to learner-facing causal attribution, especially for exercises where stochastic outcomes and deterministic bias can coexist.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Observed allele-frequency change should be compared with a null or expected distribution under chance sampling before assigning a directional mechanism such as selection.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptual; no source notation normalized
assumptions: the measured frequency change is a population-level observation
finite sampling can change allele frequencies even without fitness differences
selection predicts a biased expectation, not identical outcomes in every replicate
mutation supply may be random with respect to current advantage
multiple causes can contribute to the same observed frequency shift
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Randomness in evolutionary processes is compatible with causal explanation; it changes the kind of evidence needed before learners attribute an observed pattern to selection or another directional cause.

source_context.source_section: The Origin and Impact of Evolutionary Thought; A Synopsis of Evolutionary Theory; For Discussion and Thought

items.title: Evidence Beyond The Genetic Code
items.body: If the near-universality of the genetic code were not available as evidence, what other observations would still support the claim that all life shares common ancestry?

review.notes: Paraphrased from a Futuyma discussion prompt; useful as a coverage probe for whether the Notebook can support common descent from multiple evidence streams instead of one molecular example.

expected_answer_path: nested anatomical and developmental homologies
fossil succession and transitional evidence
biogeographic patterns
shared molecular features beyond codon assignments
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This prompt can now be paired with the universal genetic code example and the shared-derived-character evidence claim.

items.title: Why Use Effective Population Size?
items.body: If effective population sizes and migration rates are difficult to measure and define, why are they still useful concepts for explaining drift, inbreeding, and geographic variation?

review.notes: Reviewed against local OCR from the scanned Futuyma volume. Pair with the effective-population-size drift-rate claim before public promotion.

expected_answer_path: effective versus census population size
sampling variance and drift strength
migration as a counterforce to divergence
limits of estimating abstract population-genetic parameters
why a difficult-to-measure parameter can still clarify what evidence would distinguish drift, gene flow, and selection
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This turns an earlier gap into a reviewed advanced population-genetics probe. It is still best placed after learners have drift, gene flow, and Hardy-Weinberg assumptions.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Effective population size N_e summarizes how strongly drift and inbreeding behave in an idealized reference population relative to the observed census population; migration m counteracts divergence by drift.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source OCR
assumptions: sampling variance is a central route from population size to drift strength
census size and effective size can differ
population structure, sex ratio, and variance in reproductive success can affect effective size
migration can counteract divergence caused by drift
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The question requires learners to treat a mathematical abstraction as useful even when direct field measurement is difficult.

notation_mapping.meaning: effective population size
migration rate or fraction of genes introduced by migrants
population-structure measure used in the source discussion of drift and migration balance

items.title: When Is Evolution Not A Progress Claim?
items.body: A learner writes, "Evolution shows that organisms, societies, and human traits naturally progress toward better forms." Which parts of that sentence could be tested as biological claims, which parts are progress or value claims, and what evidence would be needed before treating any observed trend as a causal evolutionary pattern?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's opening historical and synopsis chapters. Use as a foundational reasoning guardrail before learners interpret trends, causal explanations, or adaptation exercises.

expected_answer_path: separate an observed biological pattern from an interpretation that ranks organisms or societies as better or higher
identify testable biological claims, such as trait-frequency change, differential survival or reproduction, origination, extinction, or directional trends in a measured trait
explain that local adaptation does not imply universal superiority, inevitable improvement, or an evolutionary goal
state that a claimed trend needs evidence about mechanism, magnitude, sampling, branching history, environmental context, and alternative explanations
distinguish causal evolutionary explanation from social, ethical, or policy justification
describe chance or stochasticity as compatible with causal analysis rather than as permission to infer purpose or progress
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a reviewed population-thinking guardrail for learners before they enter evidence-and-cause exercises, adaptation interpretation, and macroevolution trend claims.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: A directional pattern should not be treated as evolutionary progress unless the measured variable, comparison set, causal mechanism, effect magnitude, and null or alternative processes have been specified.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptual guardrail; no source notation normalized
assumptions: observed change and causal attribution are separate claims
trend descriptions require a measured axis rather than a moral or hierarchical ranking
selection, drift, constraint, extinction, sampling, and environmental change can all affect observed patterns
a cause must be capable of producing an effect of the observed direction and magnitude before it is a plausible primary explanation
biological explanations do not by themselves justify social or ethical prescriptions
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The question prevents ladder-of-life, progress, and Social Darwinist readings from being mistaken for empirical evolutionary reasoning.

source_context.source_section: Origins of Evolutionary Thought; The Impact of Darwinism; Summary; A Synopsis of Evolutionary Theory; For Discussion and Thought

items.title: Does Adaptation Guarantee Lineage Survival?
items.body: If natural selection can improve fit to local conditions, why does that not imply that species evolve to avoid extinction or that older species should always be safer from extinction?

review.notes: Reviewed against local OCR from the scanned Futuyma volume. Keep as an advanced adaptation/macroevolution coverage probe; pair with the adaptation-extinction boundary claim before public promotion.

expected_answer_path: distinguish local adaptedness from long-term lineage persistence
explain why environmental change or habitat loss may not create a new selectable route away from extinction
distinguish individual/population-level natural selection from lineage-level differential extinction
interpret constant or stochastic extinction rates without treating extinction as cause-free
explain why evolutionary trends require population- and lineage-level explanations
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This turns the earlier macroevolution/adaptation gap into a reviewed advanced synthesis probe.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Compare local adaptive change within populations with lineage-level persistence shaped by origination rates, extinction rates, and differential extinction among species.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; source discusses rates but no notation is normalized here
assumptions: extinction rate can be considered at a taxonomic or lineage level
adaptedness within populations does not necessarily lower extinction hazard
random extinction has causes but may be random with respect to the trait or adaptation being analyzed
macroevolutionary trends can arise from differential speciation and extinction
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The question asks learners to prevent adaptation language from becoming a claim of inevitable progress or guaranteed survival.

items.title: Why Does A Small Population Lose Heterozygosity?
items.body: In a finite diploid population, why does heterozygosity decline over generations even without directional selection, and how should the recurrence H_t = (1 - 1/(2N)) H_{t-1} be interpreted biologically?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's finite-population and founder-effect discussion. Use as a math-aware bridge from Hardy-Weinberg expectations to drift and inbreeding.

expected_answer_path: state that finite population size causes random sampling of parental genes
connect repeated sampling through ancestry to increased identity by descent
interpret 1/(2N) as the per-generation fraction of heterozygosity lost in the idealized case
explain that smaller N causes faster loss of heterozygosity
distinguish loss by drift and inbreeding from a selective advantage of homozygotes
connect founder events to allele loss even when initial heterozygosity may not drop as sharply as allele count
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds an explicit recurrence-based drift probe and supports learners who need to interpret equations in population-genetic prose.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: H_t = (1 - 1/(2N)) H_{t-1}; in the idealized finite diploid population, expected heterozygosity is multiplied each generation by one minus the sampling-loss term.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from OCR; source typography is imperfect
assumptions: diploid population of finite size N
random mating in the simplified model
no directional selection is required for the heterozygosity decline
migration and mutation can later add variation but are outside the simple recurrence
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Finite ancestry sampling makes descendants increasingly share genes identical by descent, so heterozygosity declines by drift.

notation_mapping.meaning: number of diploid individuals in the idealized population
expected proportion of heterozygotes at generation t
fraction of heterozygosity lost per generation under the simple finite-population model

source_context.source_section: Population Size, Inbreeding, and Genetic Drift

items.title: From Genotype Counts To Hardy-Weinberg Expectations
items.body: A diploid sample has observed counts for AA, AA', and A'A'. How would you calculate p and q from those counts, generate Hardy-Weinberg expected genotype counts, and decide whether the comparison is evidence about genotype redistribution rather than a change in allele frequency?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's Hardy-Weinberg derivation and worked moth-count example. Use as a foundation quantitative prompt before more advanced violations and extensions.

expected_answer_path: convert genotype counts into genotype frequencies D, H, and R
calculate p = D + H/2 and q = R + H/2
use p^2, 2pq, and q^2 to produce expected genotype frequencies
multiply expected frequencies by sample size to get expected counts
explain that random mating can change genotype proportions without changing p and q
treat large discrepancies as prompts to inspect assumptions rather than as automatic proof of one cause
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a reviewed calculation path for Hardy-Weinberg expectations, complementing existing assumption and usefulness prompts.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Given genotype frequencies D, H, and R for AA, AA', and A'A', compute p = D + H/2 and q = R + H/2. Under random mating and other Hardy-Weinberg assumptions, expected genotype frequencies are p^2, 2pq, and q^2.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source notation
assumptions: diploid autosomal locus
two alleles represented as A and A'
random mating
effectively large population
no selection, mutation, migration, or other force changing allele frequencies during the comparison
observed and expected counts are compared with appropriate attention to sampling error
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Hardy-Weinberg calculations separate allele-frequency accounting from the genotype proportions expected after random mating.

notation_mapping.meaning: observed frequency of AA
observed frequency of AA' heterozygotes
observed frequency of A'A'
allele frequencies calculated from genotype frequencies
expected genotype frequencies under Hardy-Weinberg assumptions

source_context.source_section: Genetic Variation and Its Origins; Hardy-Weinberg theorem

items.title: Why Use An Assumption-Bound Null Model?
items.body: If real populations almost never meet every Hardy-Weinberg assumption exactly, why is the Hardy-Weinberg model still useful for evolutionary reasoning?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's discussion prompt about the value of Hardy-Weinberg despite unmet assumptions.

expected_answer_path: state the Hardy-Weinberg assumptions
explain null-model reasoning
describe deviation from expectation as evidence of process
separate model usefulness from literal realism
coverage_probe.coverage_note: The current Hardy-Weinberg concept page should support this as a foundation-level assessment prompt.

items.title: What Can A Heterozygote Deviation Tell You?
items.body: A population sample has fewer heterozygotes than expected under Hardy-Weinberg proportions. A second sample has more heterozygotes than expected, and a third fits the expected proportions. What can and cannot be inferred about selection from each result?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's discussion prompt. Keep framed as an inference guardrail: Hardy-Weinberg deviation is evidence to investigate, not automatic identification of one evolutionary process.

expected_answer_path: compare observed heterozygote counts with the p^2, 2pq, q^2 expectation
recognize heterozygote deficiency as compatible with several causes, including inbreeding, subdivision, assortative mating, selection against heterozygotes, or sampling problems
recognize heterozygote excess as compatible with overdominance, disassortative mating, recent mixing, or sampling and scoring issues
explain that exact Hardy-Weinberg proportions do not prove absence of selection
state what additional evidence would be needed before inferring a selection pattern
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This strengthens Hardy-Weinberg coverage by moving from calculation to cautious biological interpretation.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Given allele frequencies p and q, expected heterozygote frequency is 2pq. The sign and size of observed-minus-expected heterozygote frequency are diagnostic prompts, not sufficient causes by themselves.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source discussion question
assumptions: allele frequencies are estimated from the same population sample
genotype classes are scored reliably
sampling error is considered before biological interpretation
alternative mechanisms that change genotype proportions are compared explicitly
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Hardy-Weinberg residuals are useful because they focus attention on mechanism, but the same residual pattern can arise from different evolutionary or sampling processes.

notation_mapping.meaning: expected heterozygote frequency under Hardy-Weinberg assumptions
direction and magnitude of heterozygote deviation from expectation

source_context.source_section: The Effects of Selection on Gene Frequencies; For Discussion and Thought

items.title: Hybridization And Polyploidy In Speciation
items.body: Why can hybridization and polyploidy be especially important in plant speciation, and what evidence would distinguish a new hybrid species from ordinary gene flow between related species?

review.notes: Builds on the existing Futuyma allopolyploid example and extends it into an assessment prompt.

expected_answer_path: hybridization can combine divergent genomes
polyploidy can create immediate reproductive isolation
evidence should distinguish stable independently reproducing lineages from transient introgression
plants often tolerate chromosomal changes differently than many animal lineages
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This prompt is supported by existing speciation and allopolyploid staging but may need a clearer public worked example later.

items.title: Can One Large Mutation Make A New Kind Of Organism?
items.body: A student sees a dramatic developmental mutation and concludes that a new major group could appear in one step. How would you evaluate that claim while keeping separate the size of a mutation's phenotypic effect, its likely fitness consequences, and the many other traits that define a lineage?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's discussion of saltation, macromutation, preadaptation, and developmental transformations. Use as a mutation concept-boundary prompt, not as a full treatment of evo-devo.

expected_answer_path: distinguish a mutation's genetic scale from the size of its phenotypic effect
recognize that developmental mutations can have large effects without creating a complete new lineage in one step
ask whether the altered phenotype is viable and reproductively successful in a specific environment
identify other lineage-defining traits that would still require explanation
explain that existing structures can acquire new functions without having evolved in anticipation of future need
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a mutation-focused boundary prompt for large-effect developmental variants and preadaptation language.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Mutation effect size on phenotype, mutation effect on fitness, and lineage-level evolutionary consequence are distinct variables that must be evaluated separately.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; no population-genetic equation is imposed
assumptions: mutation has an identifiable developmental or biochemical effect
phenotypic effect size does not by itself determine fitness
lineage origin requires persistence and reproductive continuity, not only an unusual individual phenotype
new function can arise by co-option of existing structures or pathways
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Large-effect mutations may matter evolutionarily, but their interpretation depends on viability, ecological context, inheritance, and later selection.

source_context.source_section: Evolutionary Novelties; Saltation or Gradual Change; Preadaptation; Regularities of Phenotypic Transformation

items.title: Adaptive Or Random Geographic Variation?
items.body: What evidence would let a learner decide whether geographic variation in a trait is mainly adaptive, mainly random drift, or shaped by migration counteracting both local adaptation and drift?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's end-of-section questions and surrounding gene-flow model discussion. Use as an advanced coverage probe after drift, gene flow, and local selection have been introduced.

expected_answer_path: compare trait variation with environmental gradients and neutral-marker variation
estimate whether migration is high enough to homogenize populations
ask whether local selection is strong enough to maintain differences despite migration
explain why drift is more plausible in small or weakly connected populations
avoid treating dispersal observations as equivalent to genetically effective gene flow
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This extends genetic-drift coverage from effective population size into the joint interpretation of drift, migration, and selection across geography.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Migration changes a local allele frequency by approximately Delta q_i = m(q - q_i); under a simple island-model balance, population differentiation by drift is reduced as 4N_e m increases.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source OCR
assumptions: population subdivision is biologically meaningful
m measures genetically effective migration rather than movement alone
N_e summarizes the population size relevant to drift
selection, migration, and drift can act simultaneously on geographic variation
neutral markers and selected traits can provide different evidence about the same populations
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The question asks learners to treat geographic variation as an inference problem involving competing and interacting mechanisms.

notation_mapping.meaning: migration rate or fraction of genes introduced by migrants
allele frequency in local population i
mean allele frequency in source or surrounding populations
effective population size used in the drift-migration balance
population differentiation measure reduced by gene flow

items.title: Are Mutations Programmed For Need?
items.body: What is gained and what is risked by describing mutations as replication mistakes rather than adaptive events programmed to meet an organism's needs?

review.notes: Paraphrased to fit the Notebook misconception guard around need-directed mutation.

expected_answer_path: mutation introduces variation before selection filters it
mutation processes can have mechanisms and biases without foresight
need-directed language can falsely imply purposeful adaptive production
selection, not mutation itself, explains adaptive fit
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This strengthens the existing mutation need-directed check with a textbook-derived prompt.

items.title: When Is Mutation Weak And When Is It Important?
items.body: A learner says mutation rates are too low to matter for evolution because the change at any one locus in one generation is tiny. How should you respond using the difference between single-locus frequency change, long time spans, population size, and mutation summed across many loci?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's mutation-pressure and mutation-rate discussion. Use as a scale-sensitive misconception check.

expected_answer_path: state that a very low mutation rate causes only small allele-frequency change at one locus in one generation
explain that mutation can create new alleles even if it is usually weak as a frequency-changing force at one locus
distinguish mutation pressure from selection, gene flow, and drift
use population size to explain why rare mutation rates can still produce some mutant offspring
use many loci and many generations to explain why accumulated mutation can matter
avoid treating mutation as a need-directed adaptive response
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a reviewed mutation scale probe that bridges molecular origin of variation with population-genetic frequency change.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: For two alleles with forward mutation rate u and back mutation rate v, the change in q can be represented as Delta q = u(1 - q) - vq; when u and v are small, per-generation single-locus change is small, but mutation input can accumulate across genomes, populations, and time.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source equation and prose
assumptions: two-allele mutation model is used as a simplified case
u and v are per-generation mutation rates
other forces can act before mutation equilibrium is reached
genome-wide mutation input depends on the number of loci and individuals considered
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Mutation is the source of new allelic variation, but its immediate effect on a single allele frequency is often small relative to other evolutionary forces.

notation_mapping.meaning: forward mutation rate from A to A'
back mutation rate from A' to A
frequency of the mutant or focal allele
change in allele frequency across a generation

source_context.source_section: Genetic Variation and Its Origins; Rates of Mutation

items.title: What Makes A Protein Clock Valid?
items.body: If neutral alleles can be used to infer how long two lineages have been separated, what assumptions must hold before amino-acid or allozyme differences can be treated as a protein clock?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's neutralist/selectionist discussion and the protein-clock prompt. Keep as an advanced drift/molecular-evolution coverage probe, not as a beginner genetic-drift definition.

expected_answer_path: identify which loci or substitutions are assumed to be effectively neutral
explain why fixation rate can track mutation rate under a neutral model
check whether effective population size, mutation rate, gene flow, and selection pressures are stable enough for the inference
distinguish constant divergence by neutral substitutions from similarity maintained by shared polymorphism or selection
state why a clock estimate is a model-dependent inference rather than a direct reading of sequence difference
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This resolves the neutral-theory context gap left by the Pianka mechanism-comparison sidecar and links drift to molecular-evolution inference.
