items.title: Does Heterozygote Success Prove Overdominance?
items.body: A study reports that heterozygotes at an enzyme marker have higher fecundity than homozygotes, and the heterozygote frequency rises over several generations. What evidence would be needed before concluding that selection is maintaining a single-locus overdominant polymorphism rather than a linked or multi-locus effect?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's treatment of heterozygous advantage and pseudo-overdominance. Keep as an evidence-quality question: observed heterozygote performance can motivate a selection hypothesis, but it does not by itself isolate the causal locus.

expected_answer_path: state that higher heterozygote fecundity or increasing heterozygosity is consistent with heterozygote advantage
explain why the marker might be linked to causal loci rather than itself overdominant
identify pseudo-overdominance as a pattern caused by linkage disequilibrium or linked deleterious recessives being masked in hybrids

coverage_probe.coverage_note: This strengthens balancing-selection coverage by adding a causal-inference guardrail around overdominance claims.

mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source prose
assumptions: fitness differences are estimated for genotypes with adequate replication
marker genotypes can be separated from linked background effects
linkage disequilibrium and recombination are considered explicitly
environmental heterogeneity and multi-locus heterosis are competing explanations
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Selection can maintain polymorphism by heterozygote advantage, but proving the mechanism requires evidence that the heterozygote advantage belongs to the focal locus rather than to the chromosomal or genetic background it tags.

items.title: When Is One Model Not Enough?
items.body: A simple model predicts that smaller, more isolated habitat patches should have fewer species because immigration is lower and extinction is higher. A survey finds that pattern, but also finds large exceptions. What should learners check before saying that patch size and isolation caused the whole result, and how should they report the simplifications built into the model?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Futuyma's preface and ecological-context chapter. Use as a model-boundary guardrail: simple models can make causal expectations explicit, but learners still need to state assumptions and rival or interacting causes.

expected_answer_path: identify the model claim as a rate-based simplification rather than a complete causal explanation
state the assumptions about source pools, immigration, extinction, area, isolation, and comparable sampling
ask whether the observed effect size is plausible under the model's specified mechanisms
look for exceptions that might be explained by succession, habitat heterogeneity, species interactions, local diversification, disturbance, or historical contingency
explain that interacting causes can produce net effects that differ from adding independent effects one at a time
report the model's useful scope and its omitted processes before applying it to real-world inference
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a reviewed guardrail for learners entering empirical exercises: simple models are useful because they specify expectations, not because they remove the need to evaluate multiple causes.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: A simplified model can predict a directional pattern, but causal attribution requires checking model assumptions, effect magnitude, alternative mechanisms, and interactions among causes.

assumptions: the same richness pattern can be consistent with more than one process

real-world explanation should state which conditions were simplified for the learning exercise
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Learners should treat model fit as a starting point for causal inquiry, not as proof that one named process explains every part of the observed phenomenon.

items.title: Soft Selection And Hard Selection

items.title: When Does Ontogeny Inform Phylogeny?

review.notes: Paraphrased from local OCR of the 1977 split-PDF scan. Use as a guardrail: developmental evidence can carry phylogenetic information, but that does not license a simplistic claim that ontogeny repeats adult ancestral stages.

expected_answer_path: state that developmental stages can preserve information relevant to common ancestry

define heterochrony as evolutionary change in the timing or rate of development of already-present characters

items.title: Why Did Population Thinking Matter For Selection?
items.body: Mayr argues that Darwin's shift from typological thinking to population thinking made natural selection intelligible. Why does selection require attention to variable individuals in populations, and why would essentialist type-thinking make that process hard to see?

review.notes: Paraphrased from local OCR of the 1982 split-PDF scan. Use as historical concept analysis rather than as a duplicate of the existing Mayr 2002 population-thinking definition.

expected_answer_path: explain that population thinking treats individual variation as biologically meaningful rather than accidental noise
connect variable individuals to differential survival and reproduction

coverage_probe.coverage_note: This strengthens the population-thinking concept by adding historical provenance for why the conceptual shift mattered.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Given a population P with variable individuals i and trait values x_i, selection can alter the distribution of x across generations when reproductive success differs among individuals; a fixed-type model cannot represent this distributional change.

items.title: What Makes A Geographic Isolate An Incipient Species?

expected_answer_path: identify a geographic isolate as a population separated from the parental population by a barrier or distance that restricts gene flow

items.title: Why Do Archipelagos Favor Radiation?

items.title: When Should Competition Produce Character Displacement?

items.title: Why Is A Jack-Of-Many-Trades Not Always Best?
items.body: A predator can evolve a narrow prey-size preference that is very efficient when that prey is common, or a broader diet that works across many prey sizes but is less efficient on any one size. ask for evidence about age-specific reproduction, mortality, density, and offspring survival before assigning an adaptive explanation
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a reviewed life-history tradeoff probe to adaptation coverage while linking to natural-selection and population-thinking guardrails.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: For a finite allocation budget, increased current reproductive effort can trade off against future survival or reproduction; the fitness consequence depends on age-specific reproductive value, mortality, fecundity, offspring size, and density context.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: qualitative source passage normalized as an age-structured tradeoff model
assumptions: time, energy, or material budgets constrain simultaneous investment in survival and reproduction
age-specific reproduction and survival differ among life-history strategies
offspring number and offspring size can constrain one another
juvenile survivorship and density context affect the value of current reproductive effort
survival is fitness-relevant only insofar as it changes reproductive output
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Life-history explanations should compare whole reproductive schedules rather than treating long life, early reproduction, many offspring, or large offspring as universally optimal.

notation_mapping.meaning: expected contribution of an individual at a given age to future reproduction
number of offspring or reproductive output in a life-history schedule
probability of surviving across a life-history interval

items.title: When Can Two Competitors Coexist?
items.body: Two species compete for limiting resources. In one model, each species depresses its own growth more strongly than it depresses the other species; in another, each species has a stronger effect on the other than on itself. Which case can produce stable coexistence in the Lotka-Volterra competition model, and why does the model treat coexistence as a condition on interaction strengths rather than just on total population size?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Pianka's competition chapter. Keep the item as a model-interpretation probe: the learner should read competition coefficients as assumptions about how each species changes the other's growth, not as direct field measurements by themselves.

expected_answer_path: identify competition as a negative interaction mediated by limiting resources, interference, or space preemption
explain that Lotka-Volterra competition adds interspecific inhibition terms to logistic population growth
interpret competition coefficients as per-individual effects of one species on the other's growth
state that stable coexistence is possible when each species limits itself more strongly than it limits the other species, after carrying-capacity differences are accounted for
contrast stable coexistence with priority-effect or exclusion cases where interspecific effects are too strong
avoid inferring coexistence from high or low census size alone without the interaction-strength assumptions
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a competition-math bridge between population growth models and ecological coexistence reasoning.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: In the two-species Lotka-Volterra competition model, stable coexistence requires the isoclines to cross so that each species has a stronger limiting effect on itself than on the competing species, with carrying-capacity ratios and competition coefficients jointly determining the outcome.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source equations and prose
assumptions: each species follows logistic growth in the absence of the other
competition coefficients summarize per-individual inhibitory effects between species
carrying capacities are defined for each species in the absence of interspecific competition
resource conditions and interaction strengths are treated as constant over the comparison
the model is a simplified equilibrium framework and does not by itself prove the field mechanism
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Stable coexistence is strongest when niche or resource differences make intraspecific limitation exceed interspecific limitation.

notation_mapping.meaning: population sizes of the two competing species
single-species carrying capacities in the absence of the competitor
competition coefficients describing interspecific inhibition

items.title: What Counts In A Population Growth Rate?
items.body: A population is increasing in census size. What additional information would you need to decide whether its per-capita growth rate reflects births and deaths in a closed population, or births, deaths, immigration, and emigration in an open population?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Pianka's demography chapter. Use to separate population-growth accounting from raw census change.

expected_answer_path: distinguish raw change in N from per-capita growth rate
state that a closed population can be summarized by r = b - d
state that an open population must also account for immigration and emigration
interpret positive r as increase and negative r as decrease under the stated accounting frame
recognize that constant per-capita r implies exponential rather than linear growth
ask whether population boundaries and movement are being measured
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a reviewed demography bridge from census change to population-level process accounting.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: For a closed population, r = b - d and dN/dt = rN under exponential growth. For an open population, growth accounting must include immigration and emigration as well as births and deaths.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source equations and prose
assumptions: population boundary is specified
birth and death rates are measured per individual per unit time
movement across the population boundary is measured or ruled out
constant r is an exponential-growth assumption rather than a default property of all populations
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Population thinking requires asking which processes change population size, not merely observing that the number of individuals changed.

notation_mapping.meaning: population size
instantaneous per-capita birth rate
instantaneous per-capita death rate
intrinsic per-capita rate of increase
instantaneous rate of change in population size

source_context.source_section: Demography; Intrinsic Rate of Natural Increase

items.title: When Can Predation Promote Prey Diversity?
items.body: A field site contains several competing prey species. After a predator is removed, one prey species becomes dominant and several rarer prey decline. What evidence would support the explanation that predation had been maintaining prey diversity, and why would it be too simple to say that predation always reduces prey populations or always stabilizes communities?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Pianka's community ecology and species-diversity passages. Keep the item focused on inference: predation can maintain diversity through density- or frequency-dependent removal, but the claim needs removal or comparison evidence and must be separated from disturbance, resource mosaics, dispersal, and other mechanisms.

expected_answer_path: identify predation as a biotic interaction that can change prey population growth and competitive outcomes
explain that predators can reduce competition among prey by selectively or randomly removing individuals
state that frequency-dependent predation can favor rare prey if predators concentrate on abundant prey types
ask for predator-removal, predator-exclusion, spatial comparison, or recruitment evidence before inferring causation
distinguish predation from other diversity mechanisms such as disturbance, resource mosaics, circular competition, and dispersal
avoid treating predator effects as universally stabilizing because predator-prey dynamics depend on response curves, time lags, prey refuges, diet shifts, and community context
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a reviewed predation-diversity probe to population-thinking coverage and connects community diversity claims to evidence and mechanism.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Predation can promote coexistence when predator impact increases on relatively common prey or suppresses competitively dominant prey enough to prevent exclusion, but the outcome depends on predator response functions, prey refuges, density context, and indirect effects.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: qualitative source passage normalized as an interaction-response model
assumptions: prey species compete strongly enough that predator removal can change competitive outcomes
predator diet or attack rate can vary with prey abundance or prey identity
rare prey have refuges, lower encounter rates, or lower predator preference relative to common prey
predator and prey responses may include time lags and indirect effects
alternative diversity mechanisms are measured or ruled out before assigning causation
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Predation should be treated as a conditional population-level mechanism, not as a blanket statement that predators either simply lower prey abundance or automatically stabilize communities.

notation_mapping.meaning: predator impact changes with the relative abundance of prey types
change in prey consumed per predator as prey density changes
change in predator abundance or reproduction as prey availability changes

source_context.source_section: Systems Ecology; Community Species Diversity; Species Diversity; Community Evolution
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: source-mined predation and diversity coverage probe

items.title: Reading A Species-Area Curve
items.body: An island taxon follows a species-area relationship in which species richness rises approximately linearly on a log-log plot of species number against island area. What do the parameters in the power-law model represent, and what does a larger slope imply biologically?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Pianka's fourth-edition island species-area discussion. Use as a math-aware ecology probe for interpreting model form, log transform, and biological meaning.

expected_answer_path: state that S is species number or richness and A is island area
state that C is a taxon/system-specific constant rather than a universal biological law
state that z is the slope on a log-log species-area plot
connect a larger z to stronger spatial replacement, habitat/topographic diversity, or island-within-island structure
explain that the model describes a pattern to interpret, not a guarantee that area alone causes richness
avoid comparing z values without considering taxa, geography, sampling, and habitat heterogeneity
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a reviewed quantitative ecology probe with explicit equation provenance.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: S = C A^z; taking logarithms gives log(S) = log(C) + z log(A), so z is the log-log slope of species richness against area.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from OCR; source line renders the exponent with OCR ambiguity
assumptions: species richness is measured consistently for the same taxon or comparable taxa
island area is measured on the same scale across observations
logarithm base does not change the fitted slope if used consistently
habitat diversity, isolation, and sampling can modify interpretation
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The species-area exponent summarizes how rapidly richness increases with area and can reflect spatial replacement or habitat heterogeneity.

notation_mapping.meaning: number of species in the focal taxon or system
island or sampled area
taxon- and place-specific fitted constant
exponent in the power law and slope on a log-log plot

items.title: What Would Show That A Community Is Species-Saturated?
items.body: Two forest habitats have similar numbers of plant species, but one has foliage spread across several vertical layers while the other is mostly a single layer. Bird species richness is higher in the vertically layered habitat. How could this pattern support a species-saturation argument, and what evidence would keep you from concluding that plant species count alone controls bird diversity?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Pianka's community ecology chapter. Keep the item focused on inference: species saturation is a hypothesis about invasion, replacement, and niche space, while bird diversity in these passages is tied to foliage structure more than plant species identity.

expected_answer_path: distinguish saturation with individual biomass or energy use from saturation with species
state that a species-saturated community would resist invasion unless the newcomer replaces an existing species
use foliage-height diversity as evidence that habitat structure can define available bird niche space
explain why plant species count alone is insufficient if bird richness tracks vegetation structure rather than plant identity
recognize that local point diversity and regional horizontal turnover can differ
define species diversity as depending on both number of species and equality of relative importance
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This extends Pianka ecology coverage from island richness and competition into community structure and diversity inference.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Evidence for species saturation asks whether added species can persist without replacement, while species diversity combines richness with the evenness or relative importance of community members.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: qualitative source passage normalized as an inference checklist
assumptions: community boundaries and habitat layers are specified
richness is separated from relative abundance or importance
the potential invading species belongs to the same relevant guild or niche space
habitat structure is measured independently of species richness
local point diversity is not treated as identical to regional turnover
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Community diversity is not just a count of species; it depends on resource space, habitat structure, relative abundances, and whether new species can enter without replacing residents.

notation_mapping.meaning: number of species in the specified community or habitat unit
how evenly individuals, biomass, or ecological importance are distributed among species
variation in foliage distribution across vertical habitat layers

items.title: Is This Individual Change Or Population Change?
items.body: A student says an individual organism evolved because it changed during its lifetime. What would you ask them to measure across generations to decide whether evolution occurred?

review.notes: Suitable as a Didactopus check that connects population thinking to allele-frequency change.

items.title: What Would Keep Diverging Populations Apart?
items.body: Two local populations differ in a visible trait. What evidence would help decide whether they are still exchanging enough genes to remain one evolutionary population, or whether barriers and divergence are moving them toward speciation?

review.notes: Use as a bridge prompt from gene flow and population structure into reproductive isolation.

items.title: What Did Wallace Infer From Nearby Allied Species?
items.body: Wallace's 1855 paper argues that closely allied species tend to appear near one another in geography and geology. How does that pattern support a historical connection among species, and why is this not yet the same as Wallace's later natural-selection mechanism?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Wallace's 1855 Sarawak Law paper. Keep as historical argument analysis: the paper supports historical affinity and biogeographic succession, but should not be backfilled with the fuller 1858 selection mechanism.

expected_answer_path: state Wallace's pattern: closely allied species tend to occur near each other in space and time
connect that pattern to historical continuity rather than independent arbitrary placement
explain why branched affinity/classification is consistent with species arising near related predecessors
distinguish evidence for historical relatedness or succession from a causal mechanism of adaptive change
name the 1858 Wallace paper, not the 1855 paper, as the existing Notebook source trail for Wallace's natural-selection mechanism
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This fills a common-descent and biogeography source gap while preserving Wallace source-family chronology.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: If related species are clustered in space and geological time, then classification, biogeography, and fossil succession can be treated as evidence for historical connection rather than independent placement.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: qualitative historical source normalized as an inference pattern
assumptions: geographic and geological observations are reliable enough to compare
taxonomic affinity reflects real similarity patterns
alternative explanations for distribution are considered
historical evidence and causal mechanism are kept distinct
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Patterns of affinity across space and time can point toward descent-like historical continuity even before a source gives a full mechanism for adaptive change.

source_context.source_section: A Law deduced from well-known Geographical and Geological Facts

items.title: Why Is One Species More Abundant?
items.body: Wallace asks learners to explain why closely related species can differ greatly in abundance even when both reproduce rapidly. How should the explanation connect population checks, food supply, enemies, and useful variation rather than fecundity alone?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Wallace's 1858 argument. Keep historical phrasing separate from later population-genetic terms; use this as a foundation-ring assessment prompt.

expected_answer_path: explain why fecundity alone does not determine abundance
identify environmental checks such as food supply, enemies, and unfavorable seasons
connect population limitation to differential persistence among individuals or allied species
explain why useful variations should tend to increase while harmful ones diminish
avoid projecting later genetic terminology onto Wallace's historical argument
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Wallace-derived historical assessment path for natural selection and adaptation.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: When reproduction can exceed replacement but population size remains bounded, differential persistence under environmental checks can change the representation of variants or allied forms.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; no modern allele-frequency notation is imposed on the historical source
assumptions: organisms reproduce in excess of long-term replacement
food supply, enemies, and seasonal conditions limit survival
variants can differ in traits affecting persistence
historical natural-selection arguments can be used without translating them into modern genetics
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Wallace's argument links abundance, scarcity, and divergence to fit with conditions of existence rather than to reproductive output alone.

items.title: When Does The Lottery Analogy For Sex Work?
items.body: Williams compares sexually produced propagules to holding several different lottery tickets rather than many copies of one ticket. In the aphid-rotifer model, what ecological and life-history conditions make that analogy a plausible selective advantage for sex?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's aphid-rotifer model. Use as an assumption-check question: the model should not be generalized to all sex without the ecological and life-history structure that makes the lottery analogy meaningful.

expected_answer_path: identify heterogonic life cycles with repeated asexual generations and occasional sexual production of widely dispersed propagules
specify small or discontinuous habitats that can be saturated by a clone
explain why many copies of one asexual genotype may be redundant if one clone can dominate the local habitat
explain why sexually produced propagules can spread a parent's genes across multiple genotypes, increasing the chance that one clone is locally best
include strong within-habitat competition and enough clonal generations for small fitness differences to change clone abundance
state caveats: high between-habitat heritability of fitness or weak local competition would reduce the advantage
avoid treating the lottery analogy as a general proof that sex is always favored
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This deepens the Williams 1975 sex-and-recombination coverage by requiring students to connect the benefit of variation to explicit ecological assumptions.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Sex can be favored in the aphid-rotifer model when diverse sexual propagules increase the probability that at least one descendant genotype wins local clonal competition enough to offset the cost of meiosis.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: source symbols normalized qualitatively; Williams uses m and n for established asexual and sexual propagules, t for clonal generations, and lambda for mean propagule number in simulations
assumptions: colonized habitats receive a limited, nonrandom sample of propagules
one successful clone can saturate or dominate the habitat
sexual propagules differ genetically while asexual propagules copy the parental genotype
within-habitat fitness differences have higher predictive value than between-habitat fitness heritability
clonal competition lasts long enough for fitness differences to affect relative abundance
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: The benefit of sex comes from distributing genetic representation across alternative local competitors when local success is winner-weighted, not from variation as an abstract good.

notation_mapping.meaning: number or average number of established asexual propagules in a colonized habitat
number or average number of established sexual propagules in a colonized habitat
number of asexual generations of competition within the colonized habitat
mean propagule number in the Williams and Mitton simulation

items.title: Benefit Or Functional Design?
items.body: A group behavior appears to benefit the group. What additional evidence would be needed before treating that benefit as the evolved function of the behavior?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's social-adaptation guardrail. Use to separate an observed beneficial effect from evidence that selection designed a trait for that effect.

expected_answer_path: distinguish a beneficial effect from an evolved function
ask whether the trait shows evidence of functional design for that effect
identify the level at which selection is being claimed to act
compare group-level explanations with individual-level mechanisms that could produce the same pattern
avoid treating group benefit alone as sufficient evidence for group adaptation
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This strengthens adaptation coverage by adding a Williams-derived guardrail for functional inference and level-of-selection claims.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Observed benefit is not sufficient evidence of adaptive function; adaptive-function claims require evidence that selection favored design features producing the effect at the claimed level of organization.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; source argument is evidentiary rather than equation-based
assumptions: a trait can have effects that were not the target of selection
group-level outcomes can emerge from individual-level adaptive behavior
functional claims require evidence beyond usefulness or benefit
the level of selection must match the evidence being used
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Learners should ask what selection process would have built the trait, not merely whether the trait has a helpful consequence.

items.title: What Must A Benefit Of Sex Overcome?
items.body: Williams frames sexual reproduction as a paradox because outcrossed sex can halve a parent's direct genetic representation compared with an asexual alternative. If a student says sex is favored simply because recombination creates variation, what additional evidence or model conditions are needed before that explanation is adequate?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's opening paradox and later recombination discussion. Use as a guardrail against hand-waving from 'variation is good' to a sufficient adaptive explanation for sex.

expected_answer_path: state the cost being explained: compared with an asexual alternative, outcrossed sex can transmit only half of a parent's genes through each sexual offspring
separate the existence of genetic variation from an explanation of why sex is favored despite the cost
ask whether the proposed benefit is large enough to offset the cost of meiosis and any recombinational load
look for life-history or ecological conditions such as uncertain environments, intense juvenile selection, colonization, or progeny fitness variance that can make diverse offspring immediately useful
avoid relying only on long-term species benefits or vague group-selection claims unless the model explains how selection maintains sex in reproducing individuals or stable mixed populations
recognize that recombination can matter under some population-genetic conditions, but those conditions must be specified rather than assumed
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds an advanced natural-selection probe for the cost of sex and for evaluating whether a proposed adaptive explanation has the right scale and mechanism.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: A sexual-reproduction advantage A is sufficient only if A offsets the direct genetic-transmission cost C and any recombinational load L; qualitatively, selection can favor sex when A > C + L under the relevant life-history and population-genetic assumptions.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: source argument normalized as a threshold model
assumptions: an asexual alternative is physiologically available or is the relevant comparison
outcrossed sexual reproduction transmits one of two maternal homologs through a given offspring
benefits of variation, recombination, or dispersal are evaluated as effects on expected descendant production
population size, mutation supply, selection intensity, and ecological uncertainty can change whether recombination or diverse progeny is advantageous
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Sex is not explained by saying that variation exists; the explanation must show why a lineage gains enough immediate or modeled expected success to compensate for the genetic-transmission cost.

items.title: When Are Identical Propagules Redundant?
items.body: Williams's elm-oyster model treats enormous numbers of seedlings or larvae competing for adult space as a setting where many copies of one genotype may be wasteful. What conditions would make genetically diverse sexual offspring a better investment than asexual copies in this model?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's elm-oyster model. Use as a threshold/model question: students should connect the advantage of sex to high fecundity, strong juvenile selection, and capped adult-space winnings rather than to variation as a generic benefit.

expected_answer_path: identify high fecundity: many seeds, larvae, seedlings, or spat enter a space that can support far fewer adults
state that juvenile competition is genetically selective rather than wholly random
explain that adult habitat has a ceiling on success, so many copies of the same winning genotype do not multiply the adult-space prize
connect sexual reproduction to higher variance among offspring genotypes, increasing the chance that at least one offspring is among the fittest few
recognize that the benefit must exceed the cost of meiosis before sex is favored
include spatial clustering or limited suitable settlement surfaces as conditions that make redundant stocking plausible
avoid applying the model where offspring are not crowded, competition is mostly nonselective, or extra copies of the same genotype produce proportional returns
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Williams 1975 model-threshold probe for high-fecundity selection and redundant stocking of capped adult habitat.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: When N juvenile propagules compete for K adult spaces with K much smaller than N, extra copies of one genotype have diminishing returns; sexual reproduction is favored if increased genotype variance raises expected capped success by more than the meiotic cost.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: source model normalized; Williams uses propagule number, local/general fitness dosage, and habitat occupancy in simulations
assumptions: juvenile propagule density is high relative to adult habitat capacity
adult space or resource capture imposes a ceiling on success for any one genotype
competition among juveniles has a genetic component
sexual offspring have greater variance in total fitness dosage than identical asexual offspring
offspring from a parent can be sufficiently clustered or numerous that duplicate genotypes compete for the same capped prize
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Sex can be favored when the limiting outcome is not how many copies are present, but whether one descendant genotype wins a crowded local contest for adult space.

source_context.textbook_prompt_role: canonical source model converted into a coverage question
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1975 monograph; local scan of third printing with corrections, 1977

items.title: Fitness Is Not One Lucky Outcome
items.body: An individual with an obvious survival impairment leaves more descendants than healthier members of its group because a chance disaster kills most of the others. Why should that lucky outcome not, by itself, make the impaired individual the fittest member of the population?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's horse-with-a-broken-leg example. Use as a misconception guard separating expected adaptive design from single-case realized reproductive success.

expected_answer_path: distinguish expected fitness from realized reproductive outcome in one case
state that chance can affect which individuals leave descendants
connect fitness judgments to survival and reproductive design relative to the population and environment
avoid defining fitness solely by one observed success after an accident
explain that repeated patterns across many cases are needed to evaluate whether the fitness judgment is wrong
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Williams-backed natural-selection probe for stochastic outcomes and expected versus realized fitness.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Fitness should be treated as a probabilistic expectation over comparable cases, while realized descendant counts can be altered by chance events in individual cases.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptualized; source argument is verbal rather than equation-based
assumptions: individuals differ in traits affecting expected survival or reproduction
environmental accidents can change realized survival independently of those traits
fitness claims are evaluated relative to a population and ecological context
single observations are insufficient to estimate expectation reliably
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Natural selection depends on biased differences in expected survival and reproduction, not on declaring every lucky survivor adaptively superior.

items.title: What Would Make Extinction Evidence Count As Group Selection?
items.body: A textbook says a trait persisted because groups or taxa with that trait were less likely to go extinct. Using Williams's criteria, what evidence would be needed before treating that as a serious group-selection explanation rather than an after-the-fact extinction story?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's chapter on sex as a factor in biotic evolution. Use as a guardrail against casual group-selection and extinction-adaptation explanations.

expected_answer_path: distinguish organic evolution within populations from biotic evolution in the composition of a biota
state the proposed group-selection claim in terms of differential persistence or extinction among groups or taxa
require evidence that extinction risk is consistently biased by the trait or life-cycle feature across comparable cases
separate a plausible story for one extinction from a predictive basis for many extinctions
recognize that local adaptation can increase fit to a niche that later disappears
avoid treating extinction frequency, extinction causes, or long-term benefit language as sufficient evidence for group selection
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Williams 1975 evidence-standard prompt for selection-level claims and adaptation/extinction guardrails.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: A group-selection explanation requires a repeatable association between group-level trait state and differential group persistence, not merely individual-level adaptation plus observed extinction.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptual criterion normalized from source prose
assumptions: groups or taxa are comparable units for the proposed claim
trait states can be measured independently of the extinction outcome
extinction or persistence is assessed across multiple cases or with a predictive contrast
within-population selection and among-group persistence are not conflated
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Selection-level explanations need evidence at the level where the claimed sorting occurs; after-the-fact extinction narratives do not by themselves establish group selection.

items.title: When Does Parent Stock Stop Predicting Recruitment?
items.body: Williams argues that some very high-fecundity populations are not well described by models where the next generation is mostly predicted from the previous generation's size. What evidence or assumptions would justify treating a population as non-Markovian in this sense, and what mistakes follow if it is modeled like a low-fecundity population?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's high-fecundity theory chapter. Use as a population-thinking/model-assumption probe, not as a blanket claim that all prolific organisms ignore parent stock size.

expected_answer_path: define the population boundary, generation interval, and recruitment measure

distinguish low-fecundity cases where next-generation size is strongly conditioned on current adult numbers from high-fecundity cases where habitat suitability and density-dependent filtering can dominate
look for weak, absent, or negative stock-recruitment relationships despite enormous zygote production
state that high juvenile mortality can still be genetically selective rather than automatically random
explain why adding young or reducing zygote production may have different effects in low- versus high-fecundity systems
avoid treating non-Markovian recruitment as universal; require evidence about fecundity, habitat capacity, density dependence, and survival filtering
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Williams 1975 bridge from sex-and-selection models to population-thinking assumptions about recruitment, habitat filtering, and fecundity.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: A low-fecundity Markovian model treats N(t+1) as strongly conditioned on N(t); a high-fecundity non-Markovian model treats recruitment R(t+1) as dominated by current habitat suitability, density-dependent filtering, and survival selection when propagule output greatly exceeds adult capacity.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source prose
assumptions: fecundity is high enough that propagule production greatly exceeds adult habitat capacity
juvenile mortality or recruitment filtering is strong
habitat suitability can vary enough to erase much of the signal from parent stock size
stock-recruitment data are available or explicitly absent
the model does not assume juvenile deaths are nonselective without evidence
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Population-level inference depends on whether reproduction or post-reproductive filtering is the limiting step; high fecundity can make habitat filtering and selective survival more important than the number of parents.

notation_mapping.meaning: population size in the current generation or census interval
population size in the next generation or census interval
number of offspring surviving to the counted or reproducing stage

items.title: How Could Heavy Juvenile Mortality Be Selective?
items.body: In a high-fecundity population, most seedlings, larvae, or juveniles die before adulthood. What evidence would let you argue that this mortality is genetically selective rather than mostly random loss, and why does that matter for natural selection?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's high-fecundity evidence chapters. Use as an evidence-design question: the point is not that all juvenile mortality is selective, but that selection cannot be dismissed without checking viability, fertility, genetic load, and gene-frequency evidence.

expected_answer_path: measure whether juvenile survival differs consistently with size, vigor, stress resistance, infection resistance, or other adaptive performance traits
ask whether the measured performance differences have a genetic component
look for genetic load or inbreeding depression as evidence that low-viability genotypes are present
compare gene frequencies across nearby localities or age cohorts where dispersal should otherwise homogenize frequencies
include fertility variation as a fitness component, not only viability
avoid assuming high mortality is nonselective merely because it is numerically large or occurs early
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This adds a Williams 1975 evidence-design probe for high-fecundity selection, linking mortality, genetic variation, and fertility to natural-selection inference.

mathematical_structure.kind: selection inference from mortality and allele-frequency evidence
mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Heavy juvenile mortality supports a natural-selection inference only when survival or fertility differences are associated with heritable variation, or when allele-frequency differences exceed what dispersal and drift plausibly explain.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: conceptual criterion normalized from source prose
assumptions: juvenile mortality is measured across comparable cohorts or environments
performance or fertility differences can be linked to heritable variation
dispersal and isolation are considered before inferring selection from spatial gene-frequency gradients
fitness includes both viability and fertility components
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: In high-fecundity organisms, enormous mortality can produce strong selection if deaths are biased among genotypes; the empirical task is to show the bias rather than assume it.

source_context.source_section: Chapters 7-8, Natural Selection in High-fecundity Populations: Evidence on Viability and Fertility
source_context.textbook_prompt_role: canonical source evidence converted into a coverage question
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1975 monograph; local scan of third printing with corrections, 1977

items.title: Why Would A Localized Clone Pay For Sex?
items.body: In Williams's strawberry-coral model, a sessile clone can spread vegetatively until environmental gradients and competitors limit it. Why might such a clone still invest in sexually produced dispersal stages even though sex diverts resources from clonal growth and pays the cost of meiosis?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Williams's strawberry-coral model. Use as an adaptation/resource-allocation and model-evidence question rather than as a claim that all clonal organisms should invest heavily in sex.

expected_answer_path: identify the organism as a sessile or localized clone that can spread vegetatively but cannot spread indefinitely
explain that environmental gradients, competitors, grazers, parasites, and local genotype-environment interactions limit clonal territory
state the tradeoff: sexual reproduction diverts resources from maintenance and vegetative spread and still pays the cost of meiosis
explain the benefit: genetically diverse dispersal stages can place some of the clone's genes into regions where a different recombinant genotype has higher local fitness
distinguish general fitness from local fitness at a position in the habitat
ask for empirical checks such as localized clone territories, limited overlap, long persistence with fluctuating borders, and rare successful establishment from sexual seedlings
avoid assuming that sexual reproduction is favored unless genotype-environment interaction and clonal localization make dispersal with variation useful
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This extends Williams 1975 coverage from isolated clonal habitats to sessile clonal organisms in environmental gradients, linking adaptation to resource allocation and local fitness.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: A localized clone can favor investment in sexual dispersal when the expected genetic survival gained by diverse propagules in new local-fitness contexts exceeds the loss in clonal maintenance, vegetative growth, and meiotic transmission.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: source model normalized qualitatively; Williams represents fitness as a combination of general and local components
assumptions: clone size and persistence depend on resource allocation to maintenance and vegetative growth
sexual propagules are dispersed beyond the current clonal territory
local habitat position changes genotype-specific fitness
boundaries of current clonal spread indicate limits of the clone's genotype in nearby conditions
genotype-environment interaction is strong enough that recombined propagules can sometimes outperform the parental clone elsewhere
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Sex can be adaptive for a localized clone when genetic diversification helps its lineage escape the finite success of one local genotype in one local territory.

notation_mapping.meaning: fitness component averaged across representative natural conditions
fitness component specific to a position in the habitat or environmental gradient

source_context.textbook_prompt_role: canonical source model converted into a coverage question
source_context.edition_or_volume_year: 1975 monograph; local scan of third printing with corrections, 1977

items.title: Discrete, Continuous, And Stochastic Population Models
items.body: When a population model is written in discrete-time, continuous-time, and stochastic simulation forms, what differences should a learner check before deciding that the models are describing the same biological process?

review.notes: Paraphrased from Wilson exercises and analytic stochastic birth-death discussion comparing discrete, continuous, deterministic, and stochastic formulations. Good coverage probe for model assumptions.

expected_answer_path: identify the time-step or event-rate assumptions
check whether population counts can go below zero or hit absorbing states
compare deterministic expectations with replicate stochastic summaries
explain why smaller time steps may improve or fail to improve agreement
coverage_probe.coverage_note: This extends population-thinking coverage from definitions into model-form comparison.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Compare discrete-time, continuous-time, deterministic, and stochastic formulations of a population growth process by tracking whether the expected change, variance, boundary behavior, and averaging procedure are preserved.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from source equations and OCR
assumptions: population size is nonnegative
time step or event-rate assumptions are explicit
stochastic summaries require replicate runs
deterministic equations describe expected dynamics or limiting behavior
a single realization of a stochastic process should not be treated as an ensemble average
different birth and death functions can share a deterministic logistic form while producing different stochastic means and variances
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Different mathematical forms can encode different biological assumptions even when they are intended to represent the same process.

notation_mapping.meaning: population size at time t
birth event function or total birth rate at population size N
death event function or total death rate at population size N
zero-mean stochastic contribution to population change over Delta t
variance in population size across the stochastic ensemble
number of stochastic runs averaged in simulation output

derivation_outline: Define the population state N(t), birth function B(N), death function D(N), and finite interval Delta t.
Write the stochastic update N(t + Delta t) = N(t) + [B(N) - D(N)] Delta t + Delta Z, where Delta Z is the zero-mean stochastic contribution.
Take an ensemble average at equilibrium to obtain the condition E[B(N)] = E[D(N)].
Use second-order birth and death functions to show that stochastic variance lowers the expected mean relative to the deterministic equilibrium when variance is nonzero.
Compute the variance contribution from possible +1 birth, -1 death, and 0 no-event outcomes over a short interval.
Compare simulation means and variances across repeated runs with the analytic expectations rather than with a single stochastic trajectory.
notebook_boundary: Keep the derivation as a model-assumption scaffold rather than a full reproduction of Wilson's algebra.
Use clean-room JavaScript if this becomes an app activity; do not port the C implementation.
Require learners to state whether they are comparing a realization, an ensemble average, a deterministic limit, or a continuous approximation.
proof_or_derivation.resolved_previous_gap: The sidecar now includes a reviewed derivation path for equations connecting stochastic updates, deterministic expected change, and variance effects.

items.title: Metapopulation Variance Across Patches
items.body: In a patch-based metapopulation simulation, how should average population size and variance across patches change as local growth, migration, or patch availability changes?

review.notes: Reviewed against local OCR and paired with the Wilson patch-variance model-output claim. Promote as an advanced stochastic simulation probe, not as a core genetic-drift definition.

algorithm_translation.translation_policy: clean-room JavaScript implementation from model assumptions and output requirements; avoid direct C port

expected_answer_path: define which patches are included in the mean and variance
separate local growth from dispersal and patch-availability rules
predict that growth-only cases relax toward carrying capacity under the stated logistic update
explain why random dispersal or switching can maintain patch-to-patch variance
use parameter sweeps or repeated runs before treating a single trajectory as biological evidence
coverage_probe.coverage_note: The prerequisite patch-dynamics material is now represented by the Wilson patch-variance claim.

mathematical_structure.formal_statement: Track mean and variance of population counts across patches as growth, migration, and patch availability parameters vary.
mathematical_structure.notation_status: normalized from exercise prose and local OCR
assumptions: patch identity and occupancy state must be defined
migration rule must be explicit
growth parameter must be separated from dispersal and patch switching
variance is measured across patches or replicate runs as specified
mathematical_structure.biological_interpretation: Spatial subdivision can make variance itself a biologically meaningful output, not just noise around a mean.

notation_mapping.meaning: local growth-rate parameter
discrete habitat unit in the metapopulation model
